data.title: $282 Million Lost From Crypto Wallet in Social Engineering Scam Triggers Monero Surge
data.description:
Crypto Wallet Loses Over $280 Million In Elaborate Social Engineering Attack

A high-value cryptocurrency wallet became the target of a sophisticated social engineering scam, resulting in the theft of more than $282 million in Bitcoin (BTC) and Litecoin (LTC).

The incident, first flagged by popular on-chain investigator ZachXBT, did not involve any protocol exploits, software flaws, or zero-day vulnerabilities.

Instead, the attacker gained access by manipulating the wallet owner through an elaborate deception.

How Did Hackers Evade Security Measures

The breach unfolded shortly before midnight UTC on 10 January, when the wallet suddenly became active.

Within hours, its vast holdings, including a substantial Litecoin balance and four-figure Bitcoin holdings, were in motion.

On January 10, 2026 at around 11 pm UTC a victim lost $282M+ worth of LTC & BTC due to a hardware wallet social engineering scam.

The attacker began converting the stolen LTC & BTC to Monero via multiple instant exchanges causing the XMR price to sharply increase.

BTC was also…

— ZachXBT (@zachxbt) January 16, 2026

The attacker moved quickly, using instant swap services and cross-chain bridges designed for speed rather than transparency.

By rapidly converting and scattering assets, the thief reduced the chances of interception or freezing.

Monero Surges Amid Laundering Activity

A significant portion of the stolen funds was converted into Monero (XMR), a privacy-focused cryptocurrency.

At the time of the transfer, XMR was trading near $450.

Within days, the privacy coin experienced consecutive all-time highs, peaking around $800 on 15 January.

XMR grew from $450 to $800 due to a hacker's money laundering 🤯

On January 10, a major social engineering attack resulted in the loss of over $282M in BTC and LTC

The attacker laundered the stolen funds by converting them to #Monero, leading to further retail purchases and an… pic.twitter.com/7uWkpAPD8I

— Web3_Vibes (@W3Vibes) January 17, 2026

Analysts noted that the price surge reflected forced liquidity demand rather than organic buying, suggesting laundering pressure.

Since the peak, XMR has retraced below $630, likely as attackers offloaded portions of the proceeds.

THORChain Posts Stir Controversy In Crypto Community

Further tracing revealed that a large portion of the stolen BTC was bridged into Ethereum, Ripple (XRP), and additional Litecoin via THORChain.

The platform’s posts on X drew swift reactions, with some community members accusing its social media team of “celebrating” criminal activity.

Others highlighted the risks of social engineering scams, warning that even hardware wallets are vulnerable when victims are manipulated by convincing fake identities.

Such scams often involve impersonation, including fake accounts claiming sudden romantic interest, even when the parties have never met.

Cross-Chain Laundering Complicates Investigation

Blockchain analysis showed that the stolen assets were fragmented across multiple chains, increasing the difficulty of forensic tracking.

Hundreds of BTC were routed through THORChain and converted into a mix of assets, slowing attribution and complicating investigations.

Analysis of the threat actor’s wallets on the blockchain shows that a significant portion of the stolen funds was swapped from BTC to ETH via @THORChain bridge. This caused unusual spikes in the protocol’s overall volumes, drawing global attention to the case.

— Whitestream - Blockchain Intelligence (@whitestream5) January 17, 2026

The case illustrates how cross-chain infrastructure, while legitimate for ordinary users, can also facilitate rapid movement and obfuscation of large-scale illicit funds.

No Signs Of State-Linked Involvement

Despite the magnitude of the theft, investigators found no evidence linking the attack to state-sponsored hacking groups that have been involved in previous high-profile crypto crimes.

The identity of the wallet owner remains undisclosed, and it is unclear whether the wallet belonged to a single individual or an institutional holder.

Threat Actor #LTC wallet: ltc1qly43c2prj4c2e85dcspzpjd36jnapnenldnr70 ltc1qpg8zkv7y7lpu70sqxkdz9sjgu3aujy9l2pkqe7

— Whitestream - Blockchain Intelligence (@whitestream5) January 17, 2026

Social Engineering Remains A High-Risk Threat

This incident serves as a reminder that social engineering scams remain one of the most dangerous threats to cryptocurrency holders.

Even hardware wallets, widely considered the most secure option for storing digital assets, are vulnerable if attackers successfully manipulate their owners.

ZachXBT’s investigation has shed light on the sophisticated methods used to bypass these safeguards, demonstrating the speed and complexity of modern crypto theft.

data.title: Trump Threatens 25% Tariffs on European Allies, Reviving Bitcoin’s Trade-War Volatility
data.description:
Trumps Threatens 10% Tariff Until He Gets Greenland

U.S. President Donald Trump has threatened sweeping tariffs on eight European countries, reigniting geopolitical uncertainty that is beginning to ripple through global markets — including Bitcoin. The president said levies of 10% starting in February, rising to 25% by June, would be imposed on Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, and Finland unless Denmark agrees to sell Greenland to the United States.

🚨 JUST IN:

President Trump drops the hammer: Starting Feb 1, 2026, 10% tariffs on ALL goods from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, UK, Netherlands & Finland for blocking U.S. Greenland acquisition!

Tick tock.
pic.twitter.com/JQmibiIPi1

— Gunther Eagleman™ (@GuntherEagleman) January 17, 2026

The announcement, delivered via Truth Social, immediately triggered emergency diplomatic meetings across the European Union, widespread condemnation from allied leaders, and protests in Denmark and Greenland, where demonstrators carried signs reading “Greenland is for Greenlanders.”

For crypto markets, the escalation has revived fears of renewed volatility reminiscent of October 2025, when Trump’s trade actions sparked one of the largest liquidation events in Bitcoin’s history.

European leaders moved quickly to present a unified front, framing the tariff threat as both economically destabilizing and politically unprecedented. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer criticized the move as “completely wrong,” warning against imposing trade penalties on allies.

French President Emmanuel Macron rejected the tactic outright, saying that “no intimidation nor threat will influence us,” while European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen cautioned that tariffs would undermine transatlantic relations and risk a dangerous downward spiral.

France is committed to the sovereignty and independence of nations, in Europe and elsewhere. This guides our choices. It underpins our commitment to the United Nations and to its Charter.

It is on this basis that we support, and will continue to support Ukraine…

— Emmanuel Macron (@EmmanuelMacron) January 17, 2026

Swedish Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson described the demand as blackmail, while Spain’s Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez warned that any U.S. attempt to force control over Greenland would “make Putin the happiest man on earth” by legitimizing territorial aggression and potentially spelling “the death knell for NATO.” Even long-time Trump supporter Nigel Farage broke ranks, conceding that the tariffs would harm European economies.

Denmark’s foreign minister expressed surprise at the announcement, noting it followed what he described as constructive meetings earlier in the week with Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio. Meanwhile, German lawmaker Manfred Weber suggested the EU should suspend its recently negotiated trade agreement with Washington, including halting zero-tariff treatment for U.S. goods.

Bitcoin absorbs geopolitical shock, volatility risk rises

Bitcoin has so far held its ground, trading near $95,000 after weeks of consolidation between $94,000 and $97,000, but analysts warn that geopolitical risk is once again weighing on sentiment. According to CryptoQuant founder Ki Young Ju, capital inflows into Bitcoin have slowed markedly, with liquidity rotating into equities and commodities, setting the stage for extended sideways movement rather than an immediate breakout.

From a technical perspective, Ledn Chief Investment Officer John Glover says Bitcoin appears to be in Wave IV of its broader bull cycle. He notes that a decisive break above $104,000 would confirm a renewed bullish phase, while a sustained move below $80,000 could open the door to a deeper correction toward the low $70,000s before the next leg higher.

The caution is informed by recent history. In October 2025, Trump’s decision to impose 100% tariffs on Chinese imports triggered a cascading sell-off across crypto markets. Bitcoin briefly plunged below $105,000, as roughly $19 billion in leveraged positions were liquidated within 24 hours. More than 1.6 million traders were wiped out, with nearly 87% of liquidations coming from long positions, while Bitcoin futures open interest collapsed by over 30%.

Policy uncertainty clouds Bitcoin’s recovery path

What makes the current episode particularly destabilizing for markets is that the tariff threat is aimed not at geopolitical rivals, but at America’s closest allies, injecting uncertainty into global trade assumptions that investors have long taken for granted.

In addition to the Greenland dispute, markets are now grappling with potential legal challenges to tariff authority, the risk of EU retaliation, and a broader escalation in U.S. trade policy spanning multiple regions.

For Bitcoin, the implications are mixed. While large holders — including institutional players and U.S. banks — continue to accumulate, thin liquidity and heightened macro risk increase the likelihood of sharp, sentiment-driven moves.

Trump’s willingness to weaponize tariffs as a negotiating tool has already proven capable of triggering violent market unwinds, and investors are increasingly wary that a similar dynamic could emerge if tensions accelerate.

For now, Bitcoin remains resilient, but the shadow of October’s liquidation cascade looms large. As trade policy once again collides with geopolitics, the crypto market is being reminded that macro shocks — not just on-chain fundamentals — continue to play a decisive role in price behavior.

data.title: A Complete Analysis of Trump's Tariff Tactics: A Practical Guide
data.description:
Author: Jesse Source: X, @jessezheng

This is an in-depth analysis from The Kobeissi Letter regarding the Greenland tariff issue and Trump's "tariff strategy."

Will the trade war reignite due to the new Greenland tariffs?

Just now, President Trump announced new tariffs on the EU and confirmed his primary strategic goal: the acquisition of Greenland. This includes a new 10% tariff on Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the UK, the Netherlands, and Finland, effective February 1st.

Furthermore, these tariffs will increase to 25% on June 1st and will not be lifted until a Greenland agreement is reached. According to Trump, this deal must be a "complete and comprehensive purchase" of Greenland.

Before analyzing our precise strategy, it must first be pointed out that the trade war has become a "cyclical headwind." Tariffs always return when the market is most surprised, and then slowly dissipate. This is a product of President Trump's "tariff strategy," and it is meticulously designed. A recent example occurred on October 10th, when President Trump threatened to impose 100% tariffs on China starting November 1st (just 21 days after the announcement). This timing may sound familiar, as it is an integral part of this strategy manual. Following the announcement, S&P 500 futures extended their intraday losses to -3.5% before the weekend close. 

October 10th - Trump threatens 100% tariffs on China

President Trump always starts with punitive and threatening messages, part of his negotiating tactics. And this tactic works well for him. The October showdown with China ended with a new trade agreement and China lifting restrictions on rare earth exports, which Trump had stated were harming the United States.

This time, the statement was released on Saturday, and market futures won't open until Monday night (because Monday is a federal holiday). The market reaction may be accompanied by a similar "emotional sell-off," but given the time to digest the news, the impact may be less severe.

This is all part of President Trump's "tariff strategy," which we will detail below: Tariff Strategy Handbook In 2025, our investment strategy yielded nearly double the S&P 500, largely because we capitalized on the asset price volatility during the trade war early on. Here are the specific strategies we have consistently employed: A Comprehensive Step-by-Step Guide to Responding to Trump's Trade War: Friday: President Trump releases a cryptic message hinting at tariffs on specific countries or industries. Markets decline as uncertainty increases. This event begins Friday with Trump's threat to impose tariffs on Denmark. Later that day or later (Saturday in this case): President Trump announces a massive new tariff, typically above 25%. Saturday and Sunday: President Trump repeatedly escalated tariff threats during the market closure to exert pressure and maximize the psychological impact. Over the weekend: Targeted countries typically respond publicly or signal a willingness to negotiate. 6 PM ET Sunday (Monday evening in this case): Futures open, and the market reacts emotionally to the tariff headlines, with futures prices falling. Monday and Tuesday: President Trump continues his public pressure, but investors begin to realize that the tariffs are not yet in effect and are still weeks away from implementation (e.g., February 1st). Wednesday of the same week: Bargain hunters intervene and trigger a easing rebound, but this momentum often fades and leads to another wave of decline. This is often when "smart money" begins to buy. The following weekend (about a week later): President Trump posted that negotiations were underway and he was working with leaders of the target countries to find a solution. Sunday evening at 6 p.m.: Futures opened sharply higher as optimism returned, but gains pared back after the spot market opened on Monday. After Monday's opening: Senior government officials, including Treasury Secretary Bessent, appeared on live television to reassure investors and emphasize progress on the agreement. The next 2-4 weeks: Officials at all levels of the Trump administration continued to provide updates on the trade agreement. Finally: The trade agreement was officially announced, and the market hit record highs.

  • Loop: Repeat from step 1.

    • Of course, this is not a 100% guaranteed roadmap, but based on our experience, almost all trade wars since January 2025 have followed roughly the same path.

    Note: This time, President Trump's plan to acquire Greenland is undoubtedly more demanding than getting China to cut export controls. Therefore, the implementation of this strategy may be more protracted, but it will follow a similar sequence of events.

    Timing is key

    President Trump's entire negotiating strategy revolves around timing and pressure. He provides a 2-3 week buffer period before the tariffs take effect in order to reach an agreement. Trump's goal is to prevent these tariffs from actually taking effect; he wants a deal. This also explains why these announcements are increasingly appearing on weekends when markets are closed. He will push the threats to the brink.

    This is why they work: if they truly take effect and persist, they will have a market-shaking and world-changing impact. In the last round of the trade war with China, President Trump announced a new trade agreement with China on November 1st—the very day the 100% tariffs were originally scheduled to take effect. Ultimately, those who were able to remain objective and follow procedures during the volatility of the trade war are enjoying the best trading environment ever. As mentioned earlier, this objective, systematic approach has enabled us to outperform market benchmarks. As shown in the chart below, since 2020, our investment strategy has returned nearly five times the S&P 500.

    Conclusion

    This time, President Trump's plan to acquire Greenland is indeed more demanding than ever before. Market volatility may last longer, but we want to emphasize our original view: the best traders are profiting from asset price fluctuations triggered by trade war headlines.

    Volatility is opportunity.

data.title: Coinbase: From a Peripheral Project to a Global Financial Infrastructure
data.description:
A recent in-depth study and analysis of Coinbase Global, Inc. (NASDAQ: COIN) covers comprehensive data, legal documents, internal communications, and market analysis up to early 2026. It examines how Coinbase evolved from a peripheral project of Y Combinator in 2012 into a behemoth controlling the global flow of crypto assets.

This article delves into the counterintuitive decisions behind its rise—seeking compliance amidst disorder; reveals the internal turmoil behind the 2020 cultural purge and allegations of racial discrimination; provides a detailed analysis of its swift and decisive actions in reshaping the regulatory environment through "money politics" in the 2024 US election; and predicts the future risks of its Web3 super-application built on the Base blockchain and its monopolistic ETF custody market.

I. The Genes of Rise: Moving Forward Through Rebellion (2012-2017)

Coinbase's success did not stem from its most advanced technology, but rather from its business strategy, which was considered the most "rebellious" at the time: in the crypto-punk world where libertarianism and anarchism prevailed, it chose to put on a suit and make peace with the banking system it was trying to subvert.

Coinbase's success did not stem from its most advanced technology, but from its business strategy, which was considered the most "rebellious" at the time: in the crypto-punk world where libertarianism and anarchism prevailed, it chose to put on a suit and make peace with the banking system it was trying to subvert.

I. The Genes of Rise: Moving Forward Through Rebellion (2012-2017)

1.1 The Roadmap Correction During the Y Combinator Era and the Origin of "Bitbank"

In 2012, when Brian Armstrong applied to join Y Combinator (YC) S12, his project was not called Coinbase, but "Bitbank". This name itself revealed Armstrong's initial ambition—not just to make a wallet, but to build a bank.

1.1 The Roadmap Correction During the Y Combinator Era and the Origin of "Bitbank" As a former fraud prevention engineer at Airbnb, Armstrong has a unique perspective. At Airbnb, he witnessed the friction and pain of funds flowing between 190 countries. He realized that while Bitcoin solved the technical problems of value transfer, its user experience was extremely poor. At the time, Bitcoin users had to use complex open-source desktop clients, dealing with 34-character hash addresses, and a slight mistake could result in the loss of all their assets. Armstrong's core insight is "Trust as a Service." In his YC application, he mentioned that at the time, there were only 100,000 Bitcoin users, and the purchase process involved multiple unreliable intermediaries. Although YC was initially skeptical of the idea (Armstrong's first application was rejected), his second application, which showcased an Android wallet prototype—along with his experience in payment security and growth hacking at Airbnb—impressed YC partners Garry Tan and Paul Graham. The addition of Fred Ehrsam was another decisive moment. As a former Goldman Sachs forex trader, Ehrsam brought Wall Street DNA that Armstrong lacked. This founding combination of "Silicon Valley geeks + Wall Street traders" instilled in Coinbase a DNA distinctly different from its competitors: rigorous, conservative, and even somewhat dull—but exactly what financial institutions needed. 1.2 Key Success Decision: Reverse Compliance Strategy 2013 and 2014 were a "Warring States period" for cryptocurrency exchanges. Japan-based Mt. Gox accounted for 70% of global Bitcoin trading volume, operating with a wild style, lacking auditing, and frequently misappropriating customer funds. Against this backdrop, Coinbase made the most important strategic decision in its history: to seek full compliance in the United States, rather than circumventing regulations through offshore registration. This decision was not only expensive at the time, but also extremely risky: Establishing banking relationships: In its early days, Coinbase not only struggled to open bank accounts, but also faced the risk of having its services cut off by banks at any time. Armstrong and Ehrsam spent considerable effort lobbying institutions such as Silicon Valley Bank to establish the first stable fiat currency channels. The Long March of Licensing: Instead of registering in a single, lenient jurisdiction, Coinbase embarked on a long "license long march," applying for money transmitter licenses in each of the 50 states in the US. This significantly slowed its early expansion but also built extremely high barriers to entry. Thus, when Mt. Gox collapsed in 2014 due to a hack and mismanagement, resulting in the loss of 850,000 bitcoins, market panic reached its peak. However, Coinbase, with its transparent reserve system and compliance structure, absorbed almost all the US traffic seeking security. This event established Coinbase's status as a "crypto safe haven," enabling it to withstand regulatory storms in the years that followed. 1.3 Early Funding and Capital Game Coinbase's early funding list was not just about money, but also about endorsement. Series A and Series B: Led by Union Square Ventures (Fred Wilson) and Andreessen Horowitz (a16z). Marc Andreessen of a16z not only brought funding, but also opened up his extensive network of connections in Washington and Wall Street to Coinbase. Strategic Investors: Coinbase brought in traditional financial giants such as the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), USAA, and BBVA as strategic investors. These endorsements send a clear signal: Coinbase is a crypto company endorsed by the "establishment."

II. Internal Warfare: Cultural Cleansing, Racial Accusations, and Moral Crisis (2018-2021)

As the company rapidly expanded, Coinbase gradually accumulated enormous tension. In 2020, this tension erupted completely against the backdrop of the racial justice movement and political polarization, leading to the most profound cultural change and public relations crisis in the company's history.

With the company's rapid expansion, Coinbase gradually accumulated enormous tension. In 2020, this tension erupted completely against the backdrop of the racial justice movement and political polarization, leading to the most profound cultural change and public relations crisis in the company's history.

Additionally, some institutions have a consensus forecast that:Subscriptions and services may reach approximately 41% of revenue in 2025 (transactions approximately 59%), while in 2020, transactions accounted for approximately 96%—this long-term trend is quite intuitive.

4.2 Stablecoin Printing Machine and ETF Monopoly

USDC, jointly issued by Coinbase and Circle, has become the core pillar of its revenue. As the Federal Reserve maintains interest rates, USDC's reserve assets generate huge interest income. Coinbase is actually enjoying net interest margin (NIM) income similar to that of banks, which is extremely stable even in bear markets. The approval of the Bitcoin spot ETF in 2024 was a crowning achievement for Coinbase's institutional business. Market Share: As of 2025, Coinbase custodied approximately 85% of Bitcoin ETF assets, including products from giants like BlackRock's IBIT and Grayscale's GBTC. Competitive Advantage: This dominant position not only brings stable custody fees, but more importantly, it embeds Coinbase into the underlying architecture of the global financial system. Now, if you buy a Bitcoin ETF on Fidelity or BlackRock, your assets are actually sitting in Coinbase's cold wallet. Coinbase's long-term narrative can be summarized as: a shift from a "high-volatility business model driven by transaction fees" to a combined model where "trading revenue remains important, but is leveled out by more sustainable service revenues such as stablecoins/staking/custody/subscriptions." Five: Web3 Deployment—Base Chain and "Super App" Ambition If Coinbase in its first decade was an exchange of the Web 2.0 era, then the future Coinbase is attempting to become the operating system of Web 3.0. 5.1 Base Chain: The L2 King That Doesn't Issue Tokens In 2023, Coinbase launched its Layer 2 network, Base, based on the OP Stack. This move marked a major strategic shift for the company.

Counterintuitive "No-Token" Strategy:Unlike Arbitrum and Optimism, which bribe users by airdropping tokens, Coinbase insists on not issuing its own token.

Compliance Considerations:Not issuing tokens avoids the risk of securities determination by the SEC.

Compliance Considerations:Not issuing tokens avoids the risk of securities determination by the SEC.

Shareholder benefits: Base's Sequencer Revenue is directly included in Coinbase's financial statements. This means that COIN stock itself becomes an "invisible token" within the Base ecosystem. Market Performance Comparison (Q4 2025 Data): 5.2 “Onchain is the new Online” Brian Armstrong put forward a grand argument: cryptocurrency is not just an asset, it is a new internet architecture. Base App (formerly Coinbase Wallet): Coinbase is transforming its wallet into a "super app" similar to WeChat. Within this app, users can not only transfer funds, but also post on social media via the Farcaster protocol, conduct automated transactions through AI agents, and even directly purchase real-world assets (RWA) on-chain. AI Agent Economy: By 2025, Base will be at the heart of the AI ​​agent economy. For example, the Virtuals protocol allows AI to have its own wallet and autonomously exchange value. Coinbase provides crucial Payment Rails for this, enabling AI to make millisecond-level payments using USDC. Part Six: Future Prospects and Risks (2026 and Beyond) 6.1 “Everything Exchange” and Neo-bank Coinbase’s 2026 roadmap reveals its ambition to become an “Everything Exchange”.

Full-category trading:In addition to cryptocurrencies, Coinbase plans to launch stocks, commodities, forex futures, and prediction market contracts. This will put it in direct competition with Robinhood, Interactive Brokers, and even the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME). National Trust Bank License: Coinbase is applying for a National Trust Bank license in the United States. Despite strong opposition from the Independent Community Bankers Association (ICBA), if approved, this would allow Coinbase to directly hold user deposits, completely bridging the gap between fiat currency and cryptocurrency, and becoming a true "crypto-native bank." 6.2 Systemic Risk: The Hidden Danger of Single Point of Failure Coinbase's success itself is becoming a risk. Custody Concentration: Coinbase controls 85% of the custodial assets of Bitcoin ETFs. This means that if Coinbase's private key management system were to be compromised or attacked by insiders (such as rogue employees), the entire US ETF market would face catastrophic consequences. This "too big to fail" concentration is highly likely to attract the intervention of the Financial Stability Oversight Council (FSOC), requiring it to accept regulatory standards similar to those for systemically important banks (G-SIBs). Fidelity's Challenge: Traditional financial giant Fidelity is actively vying for custody market share through its digital asset division, Fidelity Digital Assets. For example, MicroStrategy recently transferred some of its Bitcoin from Coinbase to Fidelity, signaling a challenge to the monopoly in the custody market. In conclusion, Coinbase's story is a microcosm of the maturing crypto industry. It proves that centralized compliance bridges still hold immense value in a decentralized world. By enduring the pain of compliance early on, decisively purging internal dissent in 2020, and demonstrating formidable political acumen in 2024, Coinbase has earned itself a ticket to the future. Now, it's no longer just an exchange; it's the gatekeeper of the on-chain world, Wall Street's digital vault, and an incubator for Web3 super applications. In 2026 and beyond, its biggest challenge will no longer be survival, but how to manage the systemic responsibilities that come with being a global financial infrastructure.

data.title: a16z: Behind the Palantirization of Everything: A Doomed Imitation Show
data.description:
Author: Marc Andrusko Translator: Deep Tide TechFlow

Deep Tide Introduction: A "Palantir-ization" craze is sweeping Silicon Valley—AI startups are emulating Palantir, sending engineers to clients' sites, providing highly customized services, and signing seven-figure deals.

Marc Andrusko, a partner at a16z, pours cold water on this trend: most companies are merely copying the surface and will ultimately become consulting firms disguised as SaaS companies. This article dissects the truly replicable parts of the Palantir model and reveals which are just beautiful illusions.

The main content: A popular phrase in startup business plans these days is, "We're basically Palantir in the X domain." Founders are enthusiastic about deploying "Forward-Deployed Engineers" (FDEs) to on-site clients, building deeply customized workflows, and operating like special forces rather than traditional software companies. This year, the number of FDE job postings has surged by hundreds of percentage points, with everyone copying the model Palantir pioneered in the early 2010s. I understand why this approach is so appealing. Enterprise clients are now scrambling to decide "what software to buy"—everything claims to be AI, and distinguishing the signal from the noise has never been harder. Palantir's selling strategy is enticing: parachute a small team into a chaotic environment, connect various self-built, siloed systems, and deliver a customized work platform within months. For startups aiming for their first seven-figure order, the promise of "we'll send engineers into your organization and get things done" is incredibly compelling. However, I highly doubt whether "Palantirization" can be adopted as a universal methodology. Palantir is a "Category of One"—just look at how its stock price trades! Most companies that copy its superficial aspects end up as expensive service companies, holding software valuations at multiples without any compounding competitive advantage. This reminds me of the 2010s when every startup called itself a "platform," but true platform companies were extremely rare because they were so difficult to build. This article aims to clarify the truly portable parts of the Palantir model and those that are uniquely unique and cannot be replicated, and to provide a more pragmatic roadmap for founders who want to combine enterprise software with high-touch services. What exactly does "Palantirization" mean? "Palantirization" initially refers to several interconnected things: Frontline Embedded Engineering: Frontline deployment engineers (internally called "Delta" and "Echo" in Palantir) are stationed within the customer's organization (usually for several months) to understand the business scenario, connect various systems, and build custom workflows on the Foundry platform (or the Gotham platform in a high-security environment). Because pricing is fixed, there are no traditional "SKUs," and engineers are responsible for building and maintaining these capabilities. A Highly Defined Integration Platform Palantir's products are not essentially a loosely designed toolkit, but rather a platform with a clear mandate for data integration, governance, and operational analytics—more like an "operating system" for organizational data. The goal is to transform fragmented data into real-time, high-confidence decisions. A High-End, High-Touch Sales Model "Palantirization" also describes a sales style: a long, high-touch sales cycle targeting mission-critical environments (defense, law enforcement, intelligence, etc.). Regulatory complexity and the scale of industry "bets" are features, not bugs. Selling Results, Not Licenses Revenue comes from multi-year, results-driven contracts, with software, services, and continuous optimization all mixed together. Contracts with a single client can reach tens of millions of dollars annually. A recent analysis defines Palantir as a "unique category" because it excels in three things simultaneously: (a) building an integrated product platform, (b) embedding elite engineers into customer operations, and (c) proving itself in mission-critical government and defense environments. Most companies can do one or two of these, but not all three. However, by 2025, everyone wants to ride the coattails of this model. Why is everyone trying to copy Palantir now? Three forces are converging: 1. Enterprise AI faces a "implementation" challenge. A large portion of AI projects stall before entering production environments, usually due to data chaos, integration headaches, and a lack of internal leadership. While purchasing intentions remain fervent (there is genuine top-down pressure at the board and C-Suite levels to "buy AI"), actual deployment and ROI often require a significant amount of hands-on work. 2. Frontline Deployment Engineers Seem to Be the Missing Bridge. Media reports and recruitment data show an explosive growth in FDE (Field Application Developer) positions this year—with different sources indicating increases between 800% and 1000%—AI startups are embedding engineers to truly enable deployment. 3. Rapid Growth Has Become the Norm (Seven-Figure Contracts Are Easier to Scale Up Quickly Than Five-Figure Contracts). If having engineers fly to the site is the price of securing a $1 million+ contract from a Fortune 500 company or government agency, many early-stage companies are willing to trade gross margins for momentum. Investors are also increasingly accepting of lower gross margins because novel AI experiences often require substantial inference costs. The bet is: you can win the client's management position and trust, deliver "results," and then price accordingly. The narrative then becomes: "We'll do what Palantir did. We'll send an elite team, build something amazing, and then turn it into a platform over time." This story can hold true in very specific circumstances. But there are some hard constraints that founders often gloss over. Where the analogy breaks down? Palantir's flagship product, Foundry, is a combination of hundreds of microservices working together to achieve a single outcome. These microservices form productized, assertive solutions to common enterprise problems across various sectors. Having met with hundreds of AI application founders over the past two years, I can tell you where the analogy breaks down: startups come in pitching a bunch of grand, outcome-based goals, while Palantir consciously builds microservices that form the cornerstone of its core competencies. This is what distinguishes Palantir from ordinary consulting firms (and why it's trading at 77 times its next year's revenue). Palantir has a suite of core products: Palantir Gotham: A defense and intelligence platform that helps military, intelligence, and law enforcement agencies integrate and analyze disparate data for mission planning and investigations. Palantir Apollo: A software deployment and management platform that autonomously and securely pushes updates and new features to any environment (multi-cloud, on-premises, offline). Palantir Foundry: A cross-industry data operations platform that integrates data, models, and analytics to drive business operations decisions. Palantir Ontology: A dynamic, actionable digital model that organizes real-world entities, relationships, and logic to power applications and decision-making within the Foundry. Palantir AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform): Connects AI models (such as large language models) with an organization's data and operations through Ontology to create productive AI-driven workflows and agents. Quoting the Everest report: "Palantir's contracts start small. The initial collaboration might be just a short bootcamp and a limited license. More use cases, workflows, and data domains are added only if the value is validated. Over time, the revenue structure shifts from services to software subscriptions. Unlike consulting firms, services are a means to drive product adoption, not a primary revenue source. Unlike most software vendors, Palantir is willing to invest its engineering time upfront to acquire meaningful clients." On the one hand, the AI ​​application companies I see now often jump straight to seven-figure contracts. But on the other hand, this is mainly because they are in a fully customized mode—they are solving any problems raised by early clients, hoping to discover themes from which to build core capabilities or "SKUs." Not every problem is a "Palantir-level" problem. Palantir's early deployments targeted areas where alternatives were "useless": counterterrorism, fraud detection, battlefield logistics, and high-risk healthcare operations. The value of solving problems is measured in billions of dollars, the number of lives saved, or geopolitical consequences, not incremental efficiency. If you're selling to a mid-sized SaaS company and helping them optimize their sales process by 8%, you can't afford the same level of custom deployment. The ROI simply can't support months of on-site engineering. Most customers don't want to be your R&D lab forever. Palantir's customers are by default accepting that they evolve the product with it; they tolerate a lot because the stakes are high and alternatives are limited. Most companies, especially those outside of defense and regulated sectors, don't want to feel like they're on a long-term consulting project. They want predictable implementation, interoperability with existing software tools, and quick results. Talent density and culture cannot be generalized. Palantir spent over a decade recruiting and training exceptionally strong generalist engineers who could write production-grade code, navigate bureaucratic systems, and sit in the same room as colonels, CIOs, and regulators. Those who left this role formed an entire "Palantir Mafia" of founders and executives. Many of these individuals are unicorns because they are both highly technical and extremely effective with clients. Most startups cannot assume they can hire hundreds of such people. In practice, the statement "We will build a Palantir-style FDE team" often degenerates into: Pre-sales solutions engineers are renamed "FDE"; junior generalists are required to handle product, implementation, and customer management simultaneously; leadership has never seen a Palantir deployment firsthand, but likes the vibe. It must be made clear that there are plenty of highly talented people out there, and tools like Cursor are enabling non-technical staff to write code. However, to successfully implement the Palantir model on a large scale, a rare combination of business and technical talent is needed, and prior experience at Palantir would be invaluable, as it is a very unique company. But the number of people in this group is limited! The service trap is real. Palantir works because there's a real platform beneath the custom work. If you only copy the embedded engineers, you'll end up with thousands of custom deployments that are impossible to maintain or upgrade. Even in a world where AI tools allow companies to achieve software-level gross margins in this model, companies that overemphasize front-end deployments and lack a strong product backbone may not be able to generate incremental returns to scale and a sustainable moat. Uncritical investors might see hockey stick-like growth from $0 to $10 million in contract value and rush in. But the question I keep asking is: what happens when dozens (or even hundreds) of these $10 million startups start crashing each other with the exact same pitch? By then, you're not "Palantir of the X domain." You're "Accenture of the X domain," just with a prettier front-end. What did Palantir really do right? If we strip away the myth, several elements are worth examining closely: 1. Platform-first, not project-first. Palantir's front-line deployment teams build on a small set of reusable primitives (data models, access control, workflow engines, visualization components), rather than writing completely custom systems for each client. 2. Clear propositions on how work "should" be done. This company doesn't just automate existing processes; it frequently pushes clients towards new ways of working, and these propositions are embodied in the software itself. This is rare courage for a vendor, and it makes reuse possible. 3. Long-Term Vision and Capital Becoming a Palantir-like company requires navigating prolonged periods of negative sentiment, political controversy, and uncertain near-term monetization as the platform and sales model mature. 4. A Very Specific Market Combination Early deployments in the intelligence and defense sectors were a characteristic, not a bug: high willingness to pay, high switching costs, high stakes, and a very small number of ultra-large clients. Not to mention a group of long-standing competitors who had secured contracts with minimal effort for decades. In other words, Palantir is not just a "software company + consulting." It is a "software company + consulting + political projects + extremely patient capital." This is not something you can simply graft onto any vertical SaaS product and generalize. A More Realistic Framework: When is "Palantirization" Justified? Instead of asking "How do we become like Palantir?", ask a series of threshold questions: 1. The Criticality of the Problem: Is the problem "mission-critical" (human lives, national security, billions of dollars) or "icing on the cake" (10-20% efficiency improvement)? The higher the stakes, the more reasonable the frontline deployment model. 2. Customer Concentration: Are you selling to a few dozen very large customers, or thousands of small customers? Embedded engineering scales better with a concentrated customer base of high ACV (Annual Contract Value). 3. Domain Fragmentation: Are the workflows of your clients similar, are they using the same software tools, or are each deployment completely different? If every client is a snowflake, it's difficult to build a consistent platform. A certain degree of homogeneity helps. 4. Regulation and Data Gravity: Are you operating in a highly regulated domain with significant data integration pain points (defense, healthcare, financial crime, critical infrastructure)? That's where Palantir-style integration work can create real value. If you're mostly in the lower left corner of these dimensions (low criticality, fragmented clients, relatively simple integration), a complete "Palantirization" is almost certainly the wrong approach. A bottom-up PLG (Product-Driven Growth) strategy is more suitable for that situation. What's Worth Learning? While I doubt every early-stage company can successfully deploy the Palantir pattern, there are a few aspects of this approach worth considering: 1. Treat frontline deployment as scaffolding, not the house. The following can be perfectly correct: Let engineers work embedded with early design partners. Get the first 3-5 customers into production at all costs. Use these collaborations to stress test your primitives and abstractions. But with clear constraints: Time-bound deployment (e.g., "90-day sprint to production")

  • Clear proportions (e.g., "maximum number of engineering staff per customer per $1 million ARR")

  • Quarterly goals for converting custom code into reusable configurations or templates

    • Otherwise, "we will productize it later" will become "we haven't had time to do it yet."

    2. Built on powerful primitives, not custom workflows. Palantir's real lesson lies in its product architecture: A unified data model and permission layer; a generic workflow engine and UI primitives; and configuration over code whenever possible. Frontline deployment teams should spend their time selecting and validating which primitives to assemble, rather than building entirely new things for each customer. Leave the new builds to the engineers. 3. Make FDE an integral part of the product, not just its delivery. In Palantir's world, frontline deployment engineers are deeply involved in product discovery and iteration, not just implementation. Strong product organizations and platform teams are fueled by what FDEs learn on the front lines. If your FDE sits in a separate "Professional Services" department, you lose this feedback loop and slide into becoming a pure service company. 4. Be honest about your profit structure. If your pitch assumes over 80% software gross margin and 150% net revenue retention, but your sales model actually requires long-term on-site projects, be transparent about the trade-offs—at least internally. For some product categories, a structurally lower-margin, higher ACV model is perfectly rational. The problem is pretending to be a SaaS company when you're actually a service company with a platform. Investors typically look at the path to maximum absolute gross profit, and one way to achieve this is with larger contracts and significantly higher COGS (Cost of Goods Sold). How I would stress test a "Palantir-like" startup. When a founder tells me, "We are the Palantir of X," my notebook is usually filled with questions like: Show me a defined platform boundary. Where does the shared product end, and where does the customer-specific code begin? How quickly does this boundary move? Walk me through the deployment timeline. How many engineers-months are needed from signing a contract to initial production use? What must be customized? What is the gross margin for a mature client in the third year? Does the investment in frontline deployment decrease "significantly" over time? If not, why? If we sign 50 clients next year, where will things go wrong? Recruiting? New employee training? Product? Support? I want to see where the model breaks down. How do you decide "not" to customize? The willingness to say "no" to customization work is often the key differentiator between product companies and "service companies with beautiful demos." If these answers are clear, based on real deployments, and architecturally coherent, then a certain degree of Palantir-style frontline deployment may be a real advantage. If the answer is vague, or if each collaboration is clearly entirely unique, it's difficult to endorse its repeatability or truly scalable potential. Conclusion: Palantir's success has created a powerful aura that dominates the venture capital startup ethos: elite teams of engineers parachuting into complex environments, piecing together chaotic data, and delivering systems that transform how organizations make decisions. It's easy to believe that every AI or data startup should look like this. But for most categories, a complete "Palantirization" is a dangerous illusion: The problems aren't critical enough; customers are too fragmented; the talent model can't scale; and the economics are quietly collapsing into a service company. For founders, a more useful question isn't "How do we become Palantir?", but rather: "How many Palantir-style frontline deployments do we need to bridge the AI ​​adoption gap in our category—and how quickly can we turn it into a true platform business?" Get this right, and you can leverage the truly important parts of this approach without inheriting the parts that will crush you.

data.title: I really wish someone had told me these things when I first started trading.
data.description:
Author: Justin Werlein & Translation: AididiaoJP, Foresight News

I've been trading for five years. During that time, I've earned nearly a million dollars and am looking forward to crossing that milestone soon. Trading has been one of the toughest journeys of my life so far, teaching me so much—about myself, about decision-making, about my relationship with money, about handling pressure, about how to distance myself from the world.

I'm writing this not to impress anyone, but because I truly wish someone had told me these things when I first started trading. Perhaps that would have saved me years of torment, fewer account blowouts, and fewer sleepless nights of self-doubt.

After countless painful experiences, I'm finally starting to succeed—and the way I did it was exactly the opposite of what I once thought I should have done. This involves multiple levels: trading psychology, strengths-based strategies, and risk management.

But to be honest, what truly changed everything for me wasn't a particular strategy or indicator, but a fundamental shift in perspective. A mental turn, a reshaping of my relationship with money. Let me explain. The Uniqueness of Trading Trading is one of the most misunderstood professions, mainly because it doesn't fit the conventional definition of "work." People often mistakenly believe that continuous action equals progress. But in trading, the opposite is true. The less you do, the better the results often are. This single idea is enough to disorient most novice traders. They treat the market like other things: starting a business, exercising, or learning a skill—always thinking that "doing more" will lead to success. In other fields, effort is visible: the time invested, the number of repetitions, the weight lifted. But trading doesn't follow this logic. The real challenge lies in the fact that 90% of the "work" in trading is sitting and waiting. Waiting for high-quality trading opportunities to clearly emerge, as if telling you, "Now is the time." This creates a paradox: we instinctively believe that the more we search, the more we find. But in reality, the more frequently you search, the more likely you are to fail. The essence of this industry is not in accumulating the number of trades, but in purposefully accumulating high-quality opportunities. The problem is not only "overtrading," but also the mindset that drives people to believe "more will lead to better results." I understand this deeply because I used to be like that. I thought that as long as I worked harder, looked at charts longer, and studied more patterns, I could crack the market code. But this path simply doesn't work. The market doesn't care about your hard work. I often see this scenario: traders staring intently at the screen, tirelessly scanning charts, firmly believing that as long as they work hard enough, the market will eventually reward them. But the more you force yourself to trade, the more likely you are to "see" opportunities where there are none. Then, all the typical mistakes follow: fear of missing out (FOMO), overuse of leverage, chasing market movements that don't fit your strategy… all stemming from an impulse to "do something," rather than patiently waiting for the market to unfold itself. The market doesn't care about your ambition. It doesn't reward hard work, only the right intentions and patience. You can spend countless hours analyzing, preparing, and trying to predict every fluctuation. But when the crucial moment arrives, the only thing that matters is: whether you are in the right place at the right time with the right mindset. And this cannot be forced. You cannot succeed in the market through brute force. What you can do is learn to identify when to act, and more importantly, when to do nothing. For me, trading takes on a deeper spiritual meaning from here. Money is worldly. When you chase it, grasp it, and try to force it to come to you, it will run away. Like holding water in your hands, the tighter you hold it, the faster it slips away. We must understand that to gain true power in life, we need to maintain a certain distance from worldly concerns. If my self-worth is entirely tied to the next profitable trade, the next day I make money, then I will be forever trapped in the hamster wheel. I must first realize that I need to step outside the game and operate from a higher level of consciousness. Viewing Trading from a Game-Playing Perspective Becoming a successful trader involves multiple aspects: psychological aspects, having a superior strategy, and strict risk management. You really need to have a statistical advantage in the market. But this is a lesson I learned at a painful cost: even with a perfect strategic advantage, a person executing the trade can ruin it all due to poor execution. Therefore, I believe that cultivating the right trading perspective is the most important thing you can do. You must treat trading like a video game. Imagine a child coming home from school and playing Call of Duty. He sits off-screen, holding the controller, free to make decisions. He observes the character's actions on the screen and reacts, but he is separate from the character—he is the operator, not the soldier charging into battle. Now contrast this with another scenario: someone who feels truly on the battlefield. Every bullet seems aimed at him, every "death" feels like his end. Many traders have no boundary between their trading and themselves. They are their own trading decisions. Every profit or loss becomes part of their identity: making money makes them feel smart, capable, and valuable; losing money makes them feel stupid, useless, and a failure. But to succeed in this game through strategic advantage and human skill, you must separate yourself from every rise and fall. How? Beware of the "daily validation" trap. First, you must understand that becoming a consistently profitable trader takes time. It's not overnight, not a week, and for most people, not even a year. If you expect to validate yourself every day or every trade, you will be trapped forever. You will make trades you shouldn't, force yourself into trades because you "need to feel like you've done something," and rigidly tie your self-worth to your account's profit and loss (P&L)—a very dangerous situation. Many traders are trapped in "survival mode": feeling like they're on an endless hamster wheel, making a good decision, then a bad one, then good again, then bad again... one step forward, two steps back, in a never-ending cycle. How can someone succeed when their mind is constantly replaying past mistakes and margin calls? Their minds are filled only with thoughts of recouping losses, proving themselves, or seeking market validation. I was once deeply trapped in this: lying in bed at night, unable to sleep, my mind replaying every bad trade. I carried the weight of each failure, as if that was my definition. I felt I had to "make the money back" to regain self-affirmation. That energy—that desperate, clinging, survival-mode energy—is poisonous. When you trade in this state, your decision-making becomes distorted: you chase trends, engage in revenge trading, and over-leverage when you shouldn't… All mistakes stem from operating out of fear, not trust. The power of forgiveness changes everything. This is why forgiveness is crucial in trading. I'm not talking about that abstract, empty feeling of "feeling good." I'm talking about true forgiveness: forgiving your blown-up account, forgiving the stupid mistakes you made, forgiving those moments you knowingly committed. You must let it all go, completely let it go. Enter the market each day as a blank slate. A brand new day, unaffected by yesterday's trades or profits/losses. If you enter today's trading with yesterday's losses, you've already lost before you even begin. If you enter this month's trading with the shadow of last month's margin call, you're not actually trading; you're trying to heal—and the market doesn't care about your wounds. You must be an observer in trading. Don't cling to your decisions, but learn from them. Remain detached, yet present; remain focused, but not desperate. Here's a spiritual principle I took years to grasp: surrender. You must surrender to the desire to control the outcome, surrender to the obsession with money. You must believe that as long as you consistently do the right thing, the results will come. And you must be able to accept that you don't know when or how it will arrive. This is difficult, really difficult. Especially when you're under pressure from bills or want to prove something. But it's the only way out. Trading Journal: An Indispensable Tool This is why keeping a trading journal is so important. It allows you to reflect afterward and ask yourself: Why did I make that decision in the first place? What did I see at the time? What was I thinking? How did the market react to my thoughts? Journaling helps you create a sense of distance. It allows you to detach yourself from the game, reviewing the game like a coach watching a replay. You are no longer a soldier on the battlefield, but a player holding the controller. You can see the essence of your decisions—it's just a move in the game, not an extension of yourself. Journaling also helps you discover your behavioral patterns. Not chart patterns, but your own behavioral patterns: Maybe you always overtrade on Mondays, maybe you always engage in retaliatory trading after losses, maybe you always can't resist adding to your position before a margin call… You can't change what you can't see, but journaling helps you see it.

Scarcity and Abundance: Two Completely Different Mindsets

This reminds me of a fundamentally changing philosophy in my trading: understanding the vast difference between operating under a sense of scarcity and a sense of abundance.

  • A sense of scarcity says: I must win this trade. I must make money today. I must prove I'm not a loser.

  • An abundance says: The market is always there. Opportunities will always come. My job is to wait for the right opportunity.

When you operate under a sense of scarcity, you are in a low-frequency, tense state. You are desperate and grasping. And the market—as a mirror of collective human behavior—will respond to you accordingly. You will make bad decisions and attract bad results.

When you operate with a sense of abundance, you trust, relax, and wait. You understand that money flows towards clarity and discipline, not despair and chaos. You must align your intentions with the market's. I cannot impose my ideas on the market. I must first react to the signals the market gives. This is not weakness, but wisdom. The market is bigger than me, bigger than all of us. My task is not to conquer it, but to dance with it. What are you truly pursuing? Reflect on your mistakes in trading; what were you truly chasing? Was it an opportunity that fit your clear framework, or simply the "feeling of making money"? This is the core question: too many traders enter the market subconsciously with the goal of "making money," while the real goal should be "establishing good trading habits." Focus on habits, and money will follow. There's an even deeper question: What does money truly represent to you? For some, money means security; for others, it means status, freedom, or proof that they haven't failed. Whatever it represents, that's what you're really pursuing when you trade. If that pursuit is tightly tied to your self-worth, your decisions will inevitably reflect that insecurity. I had to do a lot of work on my relationship with money. I had to understand that money itself is neither good nor evil; it's neutral, it's a kind of energy. It flows to where it's welcomed and runs away from where it's chased. I had to learn that my personal value has nothing to do with my account balance. I had to separate "who I am" from "what I own." Once I did that, trading became much easier. Because I was no longer trading to "feel good." I traded because I had a proven strategic advantage, and I was strictly adhering to it. The Key Shift: From Results to Process You must shift your focus from the goal of "making money in the short term" to the long-term goal of "cultivating rock-solid habits." These habits will ultimately bring you far greater wealth than you can obtain through forced trading in the short term. The key is understanding: trading success is not about how much money you make today, but about your discipline in consistently executing your strategy. The trap most traders fall into is linking satisfaction with monetary results. But in this game, money is merely a byproduct. You might make money by chance. If your satisfaction is tied to these accidental results, you are paving the way for failure. True trading success comes from the satisfaction you derive from the trading process itself—from the discipline, patience, and the ability to sit still and wait for the right opportunity amidst market chaos. The fewer trades you make, the more purposeful each trade becomes. As you begin to reduce your trading, you'll gradually understand that your goal isn't to constantly interact with the market, but to maintain sufficient patience and clarity so that you can identify and act when the market presents genuine opportunities. Patience is not passivity. A lack of patience is the fatal flaw of most traders. The urge to force action even when there's nothing to do will constantly erode your capital—both financially and mentally. Patience in trading is not passively watching market changes. It's actively choosing not to do anything when no action is necessary. It's being able to stop and analyze when you realize your emotional state is trying to take over, when a voice in your head is urging you to act impulsively: Does this decision align with my strategic strengths? When you free yourself from the need to "constantly act," you gain a different kind of power. You will no longer try to convince yourself to accept a mediocre trade. Instead, you will wait for the market to offer that trading opportunity that perfectly aligns with your strategy, fills you with confidence, and seems to be calling out to you. It is these trades that will lead to your long-term success. The ultimate truth: Ultimately, it all boils down to this: stop trading to make money, start trading to cultivate good habits. Stop chasing, start arriving calmly. Shift your focus from results to the process itself. Every time you sit down to trade, remind yourself of that long-term vision: you are not here to make a quick buck today. You are here to build a trading system that can support you for years, even decades. Less is more. Fewer trades, higher quality. Fewer decisions, clearer thinking. The market is always there, but if you keep chasing opportunities that don't exist, your capital won't stay with you forever. Train yourself to value discipline more than dollars. Success isn't about being in the market all the time, but about being in the right trade at the most crucial moments. When you feel you've put in the least "effort," that's often when you're most successful in your trading. Forgive yourself for past mistakes. Start each day with a fresh perspective. Be the player holding the controller, not the soldier on the battlefield. Synchronize your intentions with the market's rhythm. Manage your relationship with money wisely. Understand that you are not your profit or loss. And remember: you can't force the market to give you what you want. You can only react to the opportunities it offers. Surrender to the results, trust the process, and let it flow naturally. This is the game of trading. This is the realization I gained after five years and countless hardships. Now it's your turn to learn—I hope you can learn faster than I can.

data.title: X Declares 2026 the Year of the Creator With Record Earnings and a $1 Million Reward for Top Article
data.description:
The Race For The Million Dollar Article

Elon Musk has officially declared 2026 the year of the creator on X, backing the claim with a $1 million prize for a single piece of writing.

Following a 2025 fiscal year that saw record-breaking earnings distributions, the platform is pivotally shifting its financial weight toward long-form journalism and deep-dive essays.

This massive cash incentive is designed to lure high-level thinkers and journalists away from traditional newsletter platforms and back into the digital town square.

By putting a seven-figure bounty on a single article, X is attempting to prove that the written word is more valuable to its ecosystem than the viral, short-form clips that have dominated the social media landscape for years.

We’re trying something new: we’re giving $1 million to the Top Article of the next payout period.

We're doubling down on what creators on 𝕏 do best: writing.

In 2026, our goal is to recognize high-value, high-impact content that shapes conversation, breaks news and moves… pic.twitter.com/4hKBJNvNIg

— Creators (@XCreators) January 16, 2026

Why Is X Doubling Down On Long Form Content

The platform’s strategy has moved beyond simple posts.

X recently opened its Articles feature to all Premium subscribers, moving it out of the exclusive Premium+ tier to encourage a wider pool of writers.

All Premium subscribers can now publish Articles.

Long-form writing works on 𝕏.
Try it out and build your following. pic.twitter.com/Qgtnv40NRa

— Premium (@premium) January 12, 2026

To qualify for the $1 million reward, creators must produce original pieces of at least 1,000 words.

The move signals a desire to move away from "engagement farming" in reply sections and toward content that "shapes conversation, breaks news and moves culture."

By weighting Articles more heavily than short posts, the platform acknowledges that high-impact writing requires more effort and deserves a larger slice of the revenue pool.

How Does The New Payout System Work

The mechanics of how creators get paid have been completely overhauled to prioritise the "Home" feed.

Earnings are now calculated based on Verified Home Timeline impressions, meaning only views from Premium subscribers in their primary feed count toward a payout.

This effectively ends the era of making money through viral replies.

GM GM
Have you noticed something since X updated the Creator Monetization rules?
They clearly stated that impressions and engagement from Premium Verified accounts on the Home feed now count toward payouts.
Since that update, look closely at your Home timeline — most of the… pic.twitter.com/e5kJH5B5dR

— Bonafide Brand (@everythingfxx) January 18, 2026

Nikita Bier, X’s head of product, explained the reasoning behind this shift by stating,

"About half of reply spam came people gaming the rev share program in Telegram groups. Those days are now over: creators will only be rewarded for bangers that make it to Timeline."

About half of reply spam came people gaming the rev share program in Telegram groups.

Those days are now over: creators will only be rewarded for bangers that make it to Timeline.

We still have a few spam vectors left to close up, but we've eliminated two big incentives this… https://t.co/uYoWFyhPqV

— Nikita Bier (@nikitabier) January 17, 2026

This new structure ensures that only content deemed high-quality enough to be promoted by the algorithm into user feeds will generate income.

Who Is Eligible For The Million Dollar Prize

The competition comes with a specific set of rules that have already sparked debate within the global creator community.

To be in the running for the $1 million, a creator must be a legal resident of the U.S. or Washington, D.C., and at least 18 years old.

The article must be published between 16 January and 28 January 2026, and it must be original work not published elsewhere.

X has also clarified that the prize is "skill-based" rather than a lottery, with judging criteria including grammar, spelling, and technical quality, though the primary metric remains its reach among U.S. Premium users.

Can X Outperform Substack And Medium

By more than doubling its revenue-sharing pool, X is positioning itself as a direct rival to established writing platforms.

The company claims that growth in Premium subscriptions during 2025 made this expansion possible, stating, "it's only fair that we share this with our creators."

We have more than doubled the Revenue Sharing pool.

With the growth of 𝕏 Premium subscriptions in 2025, it’s only fair that we share this with our creators.

This increase unlocks significantly higher payout potential for creators across the platform.

— Creators (@XCreators) January 16, 2026

However, the "winner-take-all" nature of the $1 million prize has drawn criticism from those who believe the funds should be spread across more creators.

Unlike YouTube’s established Partner Program, which distributes billions across millions of users, X’s current focus is on high-stakes incentives to attract top-tier talent.

To support this, a more detailed earnings dashboard is being integrated into the Creator Studio to allow writers to track their performance in real-time.

NEWS: New Creator studio is now live. pic.twitter.com/5TCsKfOSgK

— X Daily News (@xDaily) December 19, 2025

The new Creator Studio on X is a dashboard for creators. It includes:

- Revenue Sharing: Track earnings from your posts.
- Subscriptions: Manage earnings from subscribers.
- Inspiration: View top-performing posts for ideas.
- Analytics: See performance metrics like impressions…

— Grok (@grok) December 20, 2025

Will These Changes Stop Engagement Farming

A major goal of this restructuring is the elimination of inauthentic interactions.

The platform has maintained that views from users on higher Premium tiers are worth more than those from lower tiers, creating a hierarchy of value for engagement.

X is also deploying AI-driven moderation and "robust measures to detect fraud" to ensure that the $1 million doesn't go to someone using bots or coordinated groups.

By focusing on "real views from Premium users," the platform hopes to foster an environment where "high-value, high-impact content" is the only way to achieve financial success.

data.title: In the AI ​​era, data is like oil. How can ordinary people monetize it?
data.description:
"Data is the new oil"—this phrase has been overused in the AI ​​community. But in the mainstream narrative, it seems to have absolutely nothing to do with ordinary people like us—it's a capital game for tech giants, a competition of graphics cards and trillions of specifications. However, after some reflection, I realized this metaphor is a very useful compass for our AI practice. I. A Seriously Misunderstood Metaphor Because in the mainstream narrative, the "data" they talk about is the entire internet, Wikipedia—petabytes of data; "oil refining technology" is tens of thousands of H100 graphics cards plus a group of scientists earning millions a year; and the "final product" is an omniscient and omnipotent God model like GPT-5. This logic is certainly sound in business, but the problem is—it's basically saying: Don't participate, you can't get on the table. We ordinary people are directly kicked out of the game. Even darker, there's another version of this story that infuriates me: Data is the new oil, and our consumer data is Venezuela's oil fields; while Meituan, Alibaba, and TikTok are like Trump in the US. They accidentally (or actually intentionally) came here to extract our data, taking it for free, refining it into "98-octane gasoline" (precise algorithms, big data price discrimination), and then forcibly selling it back to us. The result is: we become the suckers—not only contributing raw materials for free, but also being sold out and having to count the money for their platforms. In this version of the story, the only players are the giants. We lack massive amounts of data, capital, and the ability to train a large model. Therefore, "data is the new oil" becomes a slogan that sounds impressive but is utterly useless to individuals, even somewhat disgusting.

Second, a different perspective can solve this problem

I think this consensus is problematic. We need to look at it from a different angle.

If we insist on applying the concept of "data is the new oil" to ordinary people, then the problem is no longer "is this analogy correct?", but rather: How exactly does this thing guide my work?

The oil industry is so powerful because it has a very clear and inescapable logical chain: Finding oil fields (exploration) → Building refineries (processing) → Product standardization (gasoline) → Building distribution channels (gas stations) → Selling to users. For ordinary people like us, the "data oil" of the AI ​​era must also be broken down into these steps meticulously. Missing even one link will prevent your AI anxiety from turning into productivity; it will only become mental drain from "browsing news + saving links + watching others get rich." Below, I'll break down this logic for how ordinary people should proceed.

III. Step One: Where are the oil fields? — Find the "miniature gold mines" around you

In traditional industries, you have to go to places like Saudi Arabia and Russia to find oil. But with our approach, the oil fields are actually right at your fingertips. I think there are at least two main categories.

1. Personal private data: Your own backyard

This is the easiest to overlook, but the most stable type of data. It doesn't need to be large in scale, but it needs to be extremely pure.

For example, your work processes, the logic behind your decisions, the pitfalls you've encountered (failure reviews), and the unspoken rules you've learned over the years in the industry.

For example, your work processes, the logic behind your decisions, the mistakes you've made (failure reviews), and the unspoken rules you've learned over the years in the industry.

For example, your digital footprint: notes, codebases, drafts, emails… all from the past ten years count. The value of this lies in the fact that it belongs entirely to you. A “personal digital twin” or “domain expert agent” trained using this data cannot be replaced by any general-purpose large model. If you haven't used a computer much in your work and life over the past 5 years, relying solely on a mobile phone, you're unlikely to evolve into an AI producer and are destined to be an AI consumer. If you really want to make money from AI, I think you need to buy a computer. Why? Because without a computer, you're unlikely to have a systematic accumulation of data, making you a complete "oil-poor country." Don't expect to accomplish anything significant from the few pictures in your phone's album or the tens of gigabytes of voice messages and casual chat logs on WeChat—too many impurities, too poorly structured; you can't refine up to 92-octane gasoline, at best you'll get 29-octane. 2. Public Data Goldmines: Building Your "Exploration Team" The second type is data that everyone can see, but 99% of people are just "consuming" rather than "exploring": X.com, WeChat official accounts, arXiv, YouTube… these are the "public sea" of the data age. The internet, especially social media, is deteriorating too rapidly. I dare say that over 50%, possibly over 90%, of the content is AGRC (AI Generated Rubbish Content). These people are using AI to mass-produce nonsense, directly polluting the earth's crust. If you're not aware of this when you're doing geological exploration, you'll dig up nothing but garbage. Even worse: if you feed garbage to your brain or to AI, the final product will only be garbage, and might even clog your oil refinery. So, to ensure you don't dig up AGRC (Academic High-Resolution Earth), I suggest you create a rigorously selected **"inspiration source combination"**. But note: simply reading isn't enough; that's just hoarding crude oil. You need to learn **primary crude oil processing**—process each source with AI, turning them into machine-readable fuel: **Deep Sedimentary Rocks (Books)**: These are the ballast. Create a yearly reading list, including professional classics and literature. **AI Integration Method**: Don't just read mindlessly. Always use Gemini or ChatGPT to assist your reading; after finishing a chapter, let it discuss and generate thought-provoking questions. After reading, you must create electronic reading notes and feed them to AI; this is your true knowledge base. Frontier Exploration Area (Papers and Reports): Browse arXiv or Google Scholar frequently. Hold a weekly "paper luncheon" and force yourself to tackle one paper. AI Integration Method: Can't get through raw text? Feed the PDF directly to NotebookLM or ChatGPT, let it summarize the core arguments and data, turning the "hard bones" into "broth" for later use. Surface Runoff (News and Information): Use RSS or a customized information stream. I scan news headlines, and only deeply collect truly impressive ones. AI Integration Method: Don't just save links. Copy the content and let AI help you tag it, extract keywords, and categorize it for storage in your note-taking software. Otherwise, it'll just gather dust. Companion Fields (Podcasts and Lectures): Listen to TED Radio Hours on your commute. Force yourself to attend one or two offline salons each month. AI Integration Method: When you hear good ideas, don't just nod. Use Whisper to transcribe the recording into text, then let AI organize it into structured notes. Audio cannot be retrieved, but text can. High-Yield Oil Wells (Social Media): Follow a group of genuine experts on Twitter/X. Regularly clean your following list and unfollow those posting spam or negative content. AI Integration Method: When you see a great thread, directly copy it and feed it to AI to analyze the logical flaws or integrate its viewpoints into your knowledge system. Field Research (Life Observation, Field Investigation): Deliberately practice "looking at life with questions in mind." This is the kind of intuitive data that AI web crawlers can't access. AI Integration Method: When inspiration strikes, don't type; speak directly, then let AI organize it into a diary. Let AI help you transform your random thoughts into logical insights. We must cultivate the habit of readily picking up our phones and spouting off a lot of things. These six sources are your "mixed oil field." Only when your input is wild, diverse, and has undergone initial processing by AI will what you refine avoid being clichéd. Fourth, the second step: Where is the refining equipment? —Don't just focus on the big models. Having found the oil, the next step is refining it. Mainstream media keeps urging you to buy graphics cards, but for an individual, the real refinery is your own software stack + mental craftsmanship. 1. The big model is just a "boiler." Having a ChatGPT Plus membership won't make you awesome. It's like buying a boiler and standing next to it watching it shine—but you don't use it. ChatGPT, DeepSeek, and other big models are essentially basic power units, the base. They can burn, but that doesn't mean you can produce oil. 2.
